ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Rassmalai; closest parent is milk-based dessert food product (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Rassmalai; A0CKP (Milk-based desserts) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Rassmalai or its closest parent category in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Rassmalai consists of 2 soft chenna milk balls (approximately 150 g total) immersed in sweetened, saffron-flavoured thickened milk.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Rasogolla / Milk-based sweet (chenna-based dessert in sugar syrup/milk)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports values for Rasogolla (chenna balls in sugar syrup) and kheer (thickened sweetened milk). Rassmalai is a composite of chenna balls soaked in thickened sweetened milk (rabri/rabdi). Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Rasogolla and Kheer entries, weighted approximately 40% chenna ball and 60% thickened milk component, consistent with standard Rassmalai composition. Energy ~186 kcal/100g, protein ~5.5 g, fat ~6.5 g, carbohydrate ~27 g per 100 g estimated composite.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk-based Indian sweets — analogous estimate based on Rasogolla and kheer GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Rassmalai found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous milk-based Indian sweets: Rasogolla (GI ~55, University of Sydney GI Database) and kheer/rice pudding (GI ~47–54). Rassmalai is a chenna-and-thickened-milk dessert with high sugar content but significant dairy protein and fat which moderate glycemic response. GI 55 is a conservative upper-bound estimate; gi_category is low (≤55 per Veritas threshold). Glycemic load per serving = (55 × 27.0 / 100) × 150 g / 100 = 22.3 g.

hypertension.guidance: Rassmalai is low in sodium (~83 mg per 150 g serving), making it acceptable for hypertensive individuals when consumed in moderation, though its high sugar and caloric density warrant portion control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (~24 g sugar per 100 g) and significant carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rassmalai contains approximately 36 g of sugar per 150 g serving and has an estimated GI of 55; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and frequency of consumption, as the high sugar content can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation despite the moderating effect of dairy protein and fat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from full-fat dairy (~4 g/100 g) and high sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Rassmalai provides approximately 6 g of saturated fat and 36 g of sugar per 150 g serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume it sparingly, as regular intake of high-saturated-fat, high-sugar dairy desserts is associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rassmalai (also spelled Rasmalai or Ras Malai) is a classic North Indian and Bengali milk-based dessert consisting of soft, spongy discs or balls made from chenna (fresh acid-set milk curd similar to paneer), which are lightly steamed or simmered and then soaked in a rich, sweetened, thickened milk sauce (rabri or rabdi) flavoured with saffron, cardamom, and rose water, and garnished with crushed pistachios. The texture is delicate and melt-in-the-mouth, with a creamy, mildly floral sweetness. Rassmalai is a festive and celebratory dessert widely served at weddings, Eid, Diwali, and other South Asian occasions. In Singapore, it is commonly available at Indian restaurants and sweet shops, particularly those serving North Indian or Mughlai cuisine. Nutritionally, Rassmalai is energy-dense (~186 kcal/100 g), high in sugar (~24 g/100 g), and provides moderate protein (~5.5 g/100 g) and fat (~6.5 g/100 g) primarily from dairy. It is a good source of calcium (~170 mg/100 g) and riboflavin. Clinically, its high sugar content makes it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease; portion control is strongly advised. It is gluten-free and suitable for vegetarians.
common_user_queries: Is Ras Malai suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact Rassmalai entry found in ICMR-NIN published tables; nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Rasogolla and Kheer entries using a weighted composite approach consistent with standard Rassmalai composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using Rasogolla as the closest comparator. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved primary sources.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) Rasogolla and Kheer entries; no single ICMR-NIN entry for Rassmalai exists. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. GI is estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous milk-based Indian sweet (Rasogolla) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Rassmalai is available. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 40.5) / 100 = 22.3 g for a 150 g serving. The food is served at Kinara - Boat Quay (field survey data, 1 restaurant). Price SGD 7.00. Dietary fibre is low (0.1 g/100 g) as this is a dairy-based dessert with minimal fibre-containing ingredients.
